retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate indications for barbiturate use in various clinical scenarios, such as seizure disorders, neonatal withdrawal, insomnia, and induction of anesthesia. Implement safe and evidence-based practices when administering barbiturates, including proper dosing, monitoring, and adjusting for individual patient needs. Assess patients regularly for signs of adverse effects and monitor therapeutic efficacy, adapting treatment plans as necessary. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team, including primary care, emergency medicine, and specialists, to develop comprehensive management plans for patients using barbiturates. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Lorazepam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for lorazepam use based on patient presentation and clinical guidelines. Implement appropriate monitoring protocols to assess patient response and potential adverse reactions to lorazepam therapy. Select alternative treatment options or adjust lorazepam dosage based on individual patient characteristics and treatment goals. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare teams, including pharmacists and therapists, to ensure comprehensive patient care and treatment planning. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If the second dose of benzodiazepines does not abort seizures, then treat as refractory status epilepticus.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, patients who fail to respond to first and second-line therapies progress to refractory SE and require treatment with continuous IV anesthetic agents to achieve seizure control.

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure Medications -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the various classes of anti-seizure medications and their mechanisms of action. Review the indications for therapy initiation by seizure drug subclass. Describe the contraindications and adverse effects of the various agents in the seizure medication classification. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients who can benefit from therapy with seizure medications. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Generalized Tonic-Clonic Seizure -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A person experiencing tonic-clonic seizures should be rolled over to the recovery position to decrease the risk of asphyxiation and aspiration. Most generalized tonic-clonic seizures resolve spontaneously, and antiseizure medications are not usually required. Patients presenting with seizures are recommended to have intravenous access, in case of prolonged seizures. Metabolic and infectious etiologies should be investigated and treated. Seizures lasting more than 5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline consciousness meets the definition of status epilepticus. During the first 5 minutes of seizure(stabilization Phase), several interventions are necessary: maintenance of airway, breathing, and circulation; neurological examination, initiation of EEG monitoring if available; maintenance of normoglycemia; procurement of IV access; and completion of necessary laboratory work-up (electrolytes, hematology, toxicology screen, anticonvulsant levels if appropriate). Patients who are critically ill with ongoing seizures should have antiseizure medication administered intravenously to achieve therapeutic plasma levels. A benzodiazepine (intramuscular midazolam, intravenous lorazepam, intravenous diazepam, intranasal midazolam, or rectal diazepam) is the initial therapy of choice. If seizures continue for more than 20 minutes, second-line therapy should be initiated. There is no evidence-based preferred second-line therapy, but one of these three antiepileptic drugs- intravenous fosphenytoin, valproic acid, or levetiracetam, can be given as a single bolus dose.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Rapid sequence intubation should be performed in patients with impaired oxygenation or ventilation despite conservative measures.

retrieved_docs.title: Focal Onset Seizure -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the key characteristics of focal onset seizures, including their clinical manifestations and EEG findings. Implement evidence-based treatment strategies for managing focal onset seizures, considering individual patient needs and preferences. Select appropriate antiepileptic medications and other therapeutic options based on patient-specific factors and guidelines. Coordinate care transitions and follow-up appointments to ensure continuity of care for individuals with focal onset seizures. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Intravenous (IV) benzodiazepines are the agents of choice for aborting seizures emergently. The use of benzodiazepines in the prehospital setting has been shown to terminate seizures in 43% to 59% cases with SE, en-route to the hospital, with less need for respiratory support on arrival to the emergency department and need for ICU subsequently. Intramuscular (IM) midazolam has been observed to be superior and more efficacious than IV lorazepam in patients without IV access. [33] However, IM midazolam, IV lorazepam, IV diazepam, and IV phenobarbital have been recommended as initial drugs of choice for aborting seizures lasting at least 5 minutes, with similar efficacy. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure Precautions(Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Drug Management of Patients with Active Seizures
retrieved_docs.content: The drugs of choice for patient management of active seizures are the benzodiazepines. One may administer intravenous lorazepam or diazepam. If the patient has no intravenous access, alternative options include intramuscular midazolam, buccal or nasal midazolam, or rectal diazepam. Intravenous lorazepam is more effective than intravenous diazepam in inducing cessation of the seizure and preventing recurrent seizures. [13] Doses of 0.1 to 0.2 mg/kg of lorazepam administered over several minutes usually work well, and sometimes higher doses are required to stop the seizure. If the seizure persists, there is no upper limit to the dose of the benzodiazepine (despite a decreased effectiveness over time). Still, it is vital to monitor the patient for hypotension or respiratory depression.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Current guidelines recommend using continuous IV infusion of anesthetic agents for seizure suppression or burst suppression coma for 24-48 hours before attempting to wean. [2] Midazolam: Load with 0.2 mg/kg IV followed by continuous infusion at 0.05 to 2 mg/kg/hr. Propofol: 2 mg/kg IV bolus followed by maintenance infusion at 20 to 250 mcg/kg/min. Pentobarbital: 5 mg/kg IV bolus, continuous infusion at 1 to 10 mg/kg/hr. Ketamine: Load with 0.5-4 mg/kg followed by maintenance infusion at 0.3 to 5 mg/kg/hr. IV midazolam and propofol are preferred over longer-acting agents like pentobarbital as long-acting agents are associated with a prolonged need for mechanical ventilation and cardiovascular complications. [35] [36] However, higher doses and prolonged treatment of propofol have been associated with propofol infusion syndrome leading to higher mortality and complications than midazolam and ketamine.

retrieved_docs.title: New Onset Refractory Status Epilepticus -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: The following personnel should be consulted: Neurologist Anesthesiologist Intensivist

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Postresuscitation Management -- Issues of Concern -- Seizure Management
retrieved_docs.content: Acute, electrographic seizures are treated with benzodiazepines, levetiracetam, or phenytoin. Some seizures may be refractory to treatment. Providers must be aware of potential adverse effects of anticonvulsants such as cardiac arrhythmias, hypotension, and respiratory depression, and must take into consideration that any sedation induced by these medications may complicate the neurological examination. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Clonazepam -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for using clonazepam therapy. Select patients who will benefit from clonazepam therapy. Coordinate the management of a patient with clonazepam toxicity. Collaborate with the interprofessional team to monitor patients for clonazepam overdose and/or toxicity to improve outcomes and prevent the risk of developing severe side effects. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Seizure Medications -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The mainstay treatment strategy for seizures is medication management. However, much like the prescription of any other pharmaceutical agent, a clinician must balance efficacy with adverse events, and provide consideration for cost, drug interactions, patient preference, and availability. This activity outlines the indications, mechanisms of action, methods of administration, important adverse effects, contraindications, and monitoring, of various seizure medications, so providers can direct patient therapy in treating indicated disorders as part of the interprofessional team with seizure medications, with a basis on the current knowledge for optimal utilization.

retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be stabilized first with regards to their cardiocirculatory function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1467
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. These agents belong to the benzodiazepine family and share a common mechanism of enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, which produces anxiolytic, sedative, muscle‑relaxant, and anticonvulsant effects. Because they are members of the same therapeutic class, they are often considered together when selecting medication for anxiety disorders, procedural sedation, or related conditions, and their pharmacologic similarities guide dosing, monitoring, and the management of potential side‑effects in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Gabapentin, an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication, may be employed in the therapeutic management of رُهاب (phobia). Although primarily indicated for seizures and neuropathic pain, gabapentin is sometimes used off‑label to alleviate anxiety‑related symptoms associated with specific phobic disorders, providing clinicians with an additional pharmacologic option for patients who experience heightened fear responses. This potential application underscores the broader utility of gabapentin in addressing certain anxiety conditions beyond its conventional uses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2392
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol-related disorders are conditions linked to chronic alcohol consumption, and pharmacologic management may involve benzodiazepine agents. Clorazepate is a benzodiazepine that may be employed to treat alcohol-related disorders, offering sedation and seizure prophylaxis during withdrawal. Prazepam, another benzodiazepine, is also indicated as a possible treatment option for alcohol-related disorders, providing similar therapeutic benefits. Utilization of these medications should be individualized, considering efficacy, safety, and potential for dependence, to optimize patient care in the management of alcohol-related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
retrieved_docs.content: Bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics, a therapeutic group of medications used to alleviate anxiety symptoms. As members of the same drug class, these agents share the primary indication of treating anxiety disorders, although they differ in chemical structure, pharmacokinetics and side‑effect profiles, which influences the choice of a specific anxiolytic for individual patient care. Understanding that each of these drugs belongs to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, monitor for class‑related adverse effects, and provide comprehensive management of anxiety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1987
retrieved_docs.content: Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class that includes several benzodiazepine agents—bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam—as well as the non‑benzodiazepine anxiolytic buspiron. All of these drugs share the primary function of reducing anxiety symptoms, though they differ in pharmacokinetic profiles, potency, and specific clinical indications. Benzodiazepines such as bromazepam, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, flunitrazepam, midazolam, and triazolam act by enhancing the inhibitory effects of gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) at the GABA‑A receptor, leading to rapid anxiolysis, sedation, muscle relaxation, and anticonvulsant effects. Buspiron, in contrast, exerts its anxiolytic action primarily through partial agonism at serotonin 5‑HT1A receptors, offering anxiety relief with a lower risk of sedation and dependence. Recognizing that these diverse agents all belong to the anxiolytic category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the patient’s specific needs, risk of side effects, and potential for dependence.

retrieved_docs.content: are frequently used together with the antiepileptic phenytoin, suggesting combined or concurrent use of opioids and antiepileptics in pain management for patients with seizure disorders. Mefobarbital is reported to treat the onset of a generalized tonic‑clonic seizure (نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة), a seizure type encompassed by the condition “epilepsy; convulsions,” which is linked to the cerebrum (velký mozek), highlighting cerebral involvement in convulsive epilepsy. Collectively, these entities and relationships provide clinicians with a comprehensive pharmacologic arsenal to individualize epilepsy management, address comorbid anxiety, and consider multimodal therapy involving opioids and other drug classes.

retrieved_docs.content: Developmental disorders are positioned as a broader category that inversely relates to other psychiatric disorders, which encompass both اضطراب الكرب التالي للرض and رُهاب (phobic disorders). This hierarchical linkage highlights that developmental and other psychiatric disorders share overlapping clinical features and may require integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. Overall, the network of contraindications, treatment indications, and poisoning associations among CNS depressants, monoamine oxidase inhibitors, anticonvulsants, analgesics, and various psychiatric conditions emphasizes the critical need for comprehensive medication review and interdisciplinary coordination in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 814
retrieved_docs.content: 7‑nitroindazole, bromazepam, buspirone, demethyldiazepam, estazolam, midazolam, prazepam and triazolam are all classified as anxiolytics. Anxiolytics constitute a therapeutic class of drugs used to reduce anxiety symptoms, and each of these compounds is recognized as belonging to that class. Although they differ in chemical structure and specific clinical applications, they share the common purpose of alleviating anxiety, and selection among them in patient care depends on factors such as efficacy, side‑effect profile, and individual patient needs.
